title: 10 best herbal teas for upset stomach
intro_desc: Herbal teas for upset stomach are aromatic beverages prepared by steeping dried herbs in hot water. They provide relief from stomach discomfort due to their anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and carminative properties. Some popular herbal teas include chamomile, ginger, peppermint, and fennel. Chamomile tea soothes the digestive tract, reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation. Ginger tea is known for its effectiveness against nausea and vomiting, while peppermint and fennel teas alleviate abdominal cramps and gas. By consuming these herbal teas, individuals can experience improved digestion and reduced symptoms of stomach discomfort, leading to enhanced quality of life and overall well-being.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Peppermint tea helps with upset stomach because it contains menthol, a compound that has antispasmodic properties, which can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and reduce inflammation. This can alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach, such as cramping, bloating, and gas. Additionally, peppermint tea has been shown to increase bile flow, which can aid in digestion and the absorption of nutrients. The soothing and calming effects of peppermint tea can also help reduce stress and anxiety, which can contribute to digestive issues. Drinking peppermint tea can provide a natural and gentle way to find relief from an upset stomach.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic: Peppermint tea helps relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing spasms and cramping in the stomach, thanks to its active constituents, menthol and methyl salicylate.
Carminative: Peppermint tea aids in expelling gas from the digestive tract, alleviating bloating and discomfort, due to the presence of menthol and volatile oils.
Analgesic: Peppermint tea helps relieve pain in the gastrointestinal tract by blocking the sensation of pain, as a result of the menthol and flavonoids it contains.
Anti-inflammatory: Peppermint tea reduces inflammation in the digestive tract, which can help soothe an upset stomach, because of the rosmarinic acid and flavonoids present in the tea.
Choleretic: Peppermint tea stimulates the production of bile in the liver, promoting healthy digestion and speeding up the recovery of an upset stomach, due to the menthol and other volatile oils.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the peppermint plant contain menthol, which helps to relax the gastrointestinal muscles and provides relief from upset stomach and associated symptoms such as nausea and cramping.
Flowers: The flowers of the peppermint plant contain flavonoids that have antispasmodic properties, providing relief from digestive discomfort and menstrual cramps.

Stems: The stems of the peppermint plant contain rosmarinic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach.
Seeds: The seeds of the peppermint plant contain essential oils that have carminative properties, which can help to relieve gas and bloating associated with an upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, measure out 1 teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves for every 8 ounces of water.
Pour boiling water over the peppermint leaves in the teapot.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes to extract the peppermint's beneficial compounds.
Strain the tea into a cup to remove the spent peppermint leaves.
Sip the hot tea slowly, allowing it to soothe and calm an upset stomach.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm tea helps with upset stomach because it has antispasmodic properties that can help reduce the tightness and discomfort in the abdominal muscles, providing relief from symptoms such as cramps and bloating. Its soothing effect on the digestive system is also attributed to its calming properties, which can help reduce stress and anxiety that often exacerbate digestive issues. Additionally, lemon balm has been shown to have antibacterial and antiviral properties, which can help eliminate harmful bacteria in the gut that may be contributing to stomach upset. Drinking lemon balm tea can also help increase the production of digestive juices, which can improve overall digestion and absorption of nutrients.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic property: Lemon balm tea can help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing spasms and cramps that contribute to upset stomach. This is mainly due to the presence of rosmarinic acid and eugenol.

Carminative property: Lemon balm tea aids in expelling excess gas from the digestive tract, which can alleviate bloating, discomfort, and pain related to upset stomach. This is attributed to the essential oil content, including citral and citronellal.

Anxiolytic property: Lemon balm tea has a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help reduce stress-induced stomach issues. Rosmarinic acid and eugenol are responsible for this anxiety-reducing effect.

Anti-inflammatory property: Lemon balm tea can help decrease inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, which can contribute to easing symptoms of upset stomach. This is due to the presence of rosmarinic acid and eugenol, which have potent anti-inflammatory effects.

Antimicrobial property: Lemon balm tea can help inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria in the gut, which can lead to digestive issues and upset stomach. This is attributed to the essential oil content, including citral and citronellal, which have antimicrobial properties.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of lemon balm tea plant are commonly used in medicinal tea due to their soothing properties that can help alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach.
Flowers: The flowers of the lemon balm tea plant are used in medicinal tea for their calming effects, which can help reduce stomach discomfort.
Stems: While not as potent as the leaves or flowers, the stems of the lemon balm tea plant can still contribute to the relief of an upset stomach when used in medicinal tea.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 1-2 teaspoons of dried lemon balm leaves or 2-3 tablespoons of fresh lemon balm leaves.
Place the leaves in a tea infuser or teapot.
Pour 8 ounces of boiling water over the leaves and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea into a cup.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day, as needed for stomach discomfort.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: German chamomile tea helps with upset stomach because it contains antispasmodic properties that help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing intestinal cramping and promoting digestion. Its anti-inflammatory properties further soothe the stomach lining, alleviating discomfort and irritation. Additionally, chamomile tea has been shown to stimulate the production of bile, which aids in the breakdown of food and the absorption of nutrients, ultimately contributing to a more settled and functional digestive system.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: German chamomile tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like azulene and bisabolol, which help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, providing relief for upset stomach.

Antispasmodic: The antispasmodic properties of chamomile, due to active constituents like flavonoids and terpenoids, help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, alleviating stomach cramps and spasms.
Carminative: German chamomile tea acts as a carminative, helping to expel gas from the digestive tract, thereby reducing bloating, discomfort, and pain associated with upset stomach.

Mild sedative: Chamomile tea has mild sedative effects, attributed to the presence of apigenin, which can help reduce stress and anxiety, promoting relaxation and alleviating symptoms of an upset stomach caused by emotional distress.

Antioxidant: German chamomile contains antioxidants that help protect the gastrointestinal tract from oxidative stress and damage, supporting overall digestive health and reducing the risk of upset stomach.
remedy_parts: Flowers: The flowers of German chamomile are commonly used in medicinal tea due to their anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which can help soothe an upset stomach.
Leaves: The leaves of German chamomile contain volatile oils and flavonoids that can help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, providing relief for upset stomach.
Seeds: German chamomile seeds are rich in essential oils, particularly bisabolol, which has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects that can help reduce symptoms of upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 1-2 teaspoons of dried German chamomile flowers.
Pour 8 ounces of boiling water over the chamomile flowers.
Steep the tea for 5-10 minutes, depending on desired strength.
Strain out the chamomile flowers.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day as needed.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mugwort tea helps with upset stomach because it contains anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help soothe and relax the digestive tract, reducing symptoms of indigestion, bloating, and cramping. Additionally, mugwort has been shown to stimulate the production of digestive juices, which can help break down food and improve overall digestion. The tea's mild sedative effects may also help alleviate stress and anxiety that can exacerbate digestive issues, providing further relief to an upset stomach.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic: Mugwort tea helps relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing painful spasms and cramps that often accompany an upset stomach. This is due to the presence of volatile oils like camphor, which has a relaxing effect on the smooth muscles.

Carminative: Mugwort tea promotes the expulsion of intestinal gas, reducing bloating and discomfort associated with upset stomachs. The active constituents responsible for this property include volatile oils like cineole and flavonoids, which stimulate the digestive system and help eliminate excess gas.

Choleretic: Mugwort tea increases the production of bile in the liver, which aids in the digestion of fats and can help alleviate symptoms of upset stomach. The active constituents responsible for this property include flavonoids and terpenes, which stimulate bile production and flow.

Antiemetic: Mugwort tea helps alleviate nausea and vomiting, common symptoms of an upset stomach. This is due to the presence of volatile oils like camphor and cineole, which have a soothing effect on the stomach lining and help reduce feelings of nausea.

Anxiolytic: Mugwort tea has mild sedative and anxiety-reducing properties, which can help alleviate stress-related digestive issues. This is due to the presence of flavonoids and volatile oils, which have a calming effect on the central nervous system and can help reduce tension in the gastrointestinal tract.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of mugwort tea plant are commonly used in medicinal tea due to their antispasmodic properties, which can help soothe an upset stomach.
Rhyzomes: Mugwort rhyzomes are rich in volatile oils and have been used in tea to help stimulate digestion and alleviate symptoms of upset stomach.
Flowers: The flowers of the mugwort tea plant contain flavonoids and other compounds that have anti-inflammatory properties, making them useful in tea to soothe an upset stomach.
Seeds: Mugwort seeds are a good source of essential oils and have been used in tea to help relieve digestive discomfort and promote appetite.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool down for a minute or two, so it's not boiling hot when you add the mugwort.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried mugwort leaves to a tea infuser or a reusable tea bag.
Pour the hot water over the mugwort leaves and let them steep for 5-10 minutes, depending on how strong you want the tea to be.
Remove the tea infuser or tea bag and discard the used mugwort leaves.
Enjoy your freshly brewed mugwort tea, preferably without adding any sugar or sweeteners, as they may interfere with the herb's natural healing properties.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Fennel tea helps with upset stomach because it contains volatile oils, such as anethole, fenchone, and estragole, which have antispasmodic and carminative properties. These compounds help to relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing colon spasms and gas production, thereby alleviating symptoms of bloating, gas, and cramps. Additionally, fennel tea has been shown to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, promoting proper digestion and nutrient absorption, which can further help to soothe an upset stomach. Its anti-inflammatory and soothing properties can also help to heal stomach lining, providing relief from discomfort and irritation.
remedy_properties: Carminative: Fennel tea can help expel gas from the stomach and intestines, reducing bloating and discomfort associated with upset stomach. This property is important because it helps alleviate one of the most common symptoms of upset stomach. The active constituents responsible for this property are volatile oils such as anethole, fenchone, and estragole.

Antispasmodic: Fennel tea can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing cramping and pain associated with upset stomach. This property is important because it can help alleviate the discomfort caused by spasms in the digestive tract. The active constituents responsible for this property are volatile oils and rosmarinic acid.

Anti-inflammatory: Fennel tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can contribute to an upset stomach. This property is important because it can help soothe and heal the digestive tract, reducing symptoms of upset stomach. The active constituents responsible for this property are volatile oils, flavonoids, and phenolic acids.

Diuretic: Fennel tea can help increase urine production, which can help remove toxins from the body and reduce fluid retention in the abdomen. This property is important because it can help alleviate symptoms of bloating and discomfort associated with upset stomach. The active constituents responsible for this property are volatile oils and potassium.

Digestive stimulant: Fennel tea can help stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, improving digestion and nutrient absorption. This property is important because it can help prevent the onset of upset stomach by promoting healthy digestion. The active constituents responsible for this property are volatile oils, particularly anethole and fenchone.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Fennel seeds are commonly used in medicinal tea to soothe upset stomachs due to their carminative and antispasmodic properties.
Leaves: The leaves of the fennel plant are used in tea to aid digestion and alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach, as they contain volatile oils with calming effects.
Rhyzomes: Fennel rhyzomes, also known as underground stems, are rich in compounds that help reduce inflammation and relax gastrointestinal muscles, making them useful for upset stomach relief.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 1 teaspoon of fennel seeds and gently crush them using a mortar and pestle.
Place the crushed fennel seeds in a tea infuser or loose in a teapot.
Boil 1 cup of water and pour it over the fennel seeds.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea, discard the fennel seeds, and enjoy the herbal fennel tea while it's still warm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Spearmint tea helps with upset stomach because it contains menthol, a compound with antispasmodic properties that can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and alleviate symptoms of indigestion, gas, and bloating. Additionally, spearmint has carminative effects, which means it can help expel gas from the stomach and intestines, providing relief from discomfort and pain. Furthermore, spearmint tea is caffeine-free and hydrating, making it a gentle and soothing beverage for an upset stomach. Its refreshing and mildly sweet flavor also makes it a pleasant and comforting choice for those experiencing digestive issues.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic: Spearmint tea helps relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing spasms and cramping, which can alleviate discomfort and pain in the stomach. This property is due to the presence of menthol and menthone.

Carminative: Spearmint tea aids in expelling gas from the stomach and intestines, which can help reduce bloating, discomfort, and pain caused by excess gas buildup. This is attributed to the volatile oils, particularly menthol and carvone.
Antiemetic: Spearmint tea can help alleviate nausea and vomiting, which are common symptoms of an upset stomach. Rosmarinic acid and menthol contribute to this property.

Anti-inflammatory: Spearmint tea has anti-inflammatory effects that can help reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, which can help soothe and heal an upset stomach. Rosmarinic acid and flavonoids are responsible for this property.
Antibacterial: Spearmint tea can help inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria in the stomach, which can contribute to an upset stomach. The primary antibacterial compound in spearmint is carvone.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Spearmint leaves are commonly used in medicinal tea for upset stomach due to their calming and soothing properties.
Flowers: Spearmint flowers contain flavonoids that help reduce inflammation, making them useful for treating digestive issues.
Seeds: Spearmint seeds are rich in antioxidants and can help alleviate symptoms of upset stomach, such as bloating and cramping.
remedy_recipe: Measure out 1 teaspoon of dried spearmint leaves or 1 tablespoon of fresh spearmint leaves.
Place the spearmint leaves in a tea infuser or teapot.
Pour 8 ounces of boiling water over the spearmint leaves.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea, then enjoy.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice tea helps with upset stomach because it contains active compounds that can help soothe digestive problems. One of these compounds, glycyrrhizin, has been shown to increase the mucus lining of the stomach, protecting it from acid and irritation. Additionally, licorice tea may help reduce inflammation in the stomach and intestines, providing relief from symptoms such as bloating, cramping, and nausea. Furthermore, licorice tea can stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, promoting healthy digestion and absorption of nutrients. Overall, licorice tea's soothing and anti-inflammatory properties make it a natural and effective remedy for upset stomach.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Licorice tea can reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, which is important for upset stomach as it can help alleviate symptoms such as pain and discomfort caused by inflammation. The active constituents responsible for this property are glycyrrhizin and flavonoids.

Mucosal protective: Licorice tea can help protect and heal the stomach lining, which is crucial for upset stomach as it can prevent and treat stomach ulcers and other forms of damage. The active constituent responsible for this property is glycyrrhizin.

Antispasmodic: Licorice tea can relieve smooth muscle spasms, which is important for upset stomach as it can help reduce cramping and pain in the stomach. The active constituent responsible for this property is glycyrrhetinic acid.

Demulcent: Licorice tea can form a soothing film over mucous membranes, which is important for upset stomach as it can help alleviate irritation and inflammation in the stomach. The active constituents responsible for this property are polysaccharides and flavonoids.

Antacid: Licorice tea can neutralize stomach acid, which is important for upset stomach as it can help reduce symptoms such as heartburn and indigestion. The active constituent responsible for this property is glycyrrhizin.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice tea plant are commonly used in medicinal tea for upset stomach due to their anti-inflammatory and soothing properties.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice tea plant are used in medicinal tea to treat upset stomach, as they contain flavonoids that help reduce inflammation and irritation.
Seeds: The seeds of the licorice tea plant are used in medicinal tea for upset stomach because they contain glycyrrhizin, which has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and stomach-soothing effects.

Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the licorice tea plant are often used in medicinal tea for upset stomach due to their ability to stimulate the production of mucus in the stomach, which can protect the stomach lining.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, boil 8 ounces of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the licorice root.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your upset stomach.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Eureka tea helps with upset stomach because it contains natural herbs that have anti-inflammatory and soothing properties. Peppermint and ginger, two common ingredients in this tea, have been shown to alleviate digestive discomforts such as nausea, bloating, and cramps. Peppermint oil helps to relax the gastrointestinal muscles, which can ease the process of digestion and reduce pain. Ginger, on the other hand, is known to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, which helps break down food and move it through the digestive tract more smoothly. The combined effects of these herbs make Eureka tea a comforting and effective remedy for an upset stomach.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Eureka tea's anti-inflammatory property, due to the presence of flavonoids and tannins, helps reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, which can alleviate discomfort caused by upset stomach.
Antispasmodic: The antispasmodic property of Eureka tea, attributed to its volatile oils, helps relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing cramps and pain associated with an upset stomach.

Astringent: Eureka tea's astringent property, resulting from tannins, helps constrict and tighten the tissues of the stomach lining, which can help protect it from irritation and reduce symptoms of an upset stomach.
Carminative: The carminative property of Eureka tea, owing to its essential oils, aids in expelling excess gas from the digestive tract, relieving bloating and discomfort caused by an upset stomach.

Antioxidant: Eureka tea's antioxidant property, attributed to its polyphenols, helps protect the stomach lining from oxidative stress and damage, supporting overall digestive health and helping to prevent upset stomach.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the Eureka tea plant contain compounds that can help soothe an upset stomach by reducing inflammation and relaxing the gastrointestinal muscles.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of the Eureka tea plant have been traditionally used to treat digestive issues, including upset stomach, due to their anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the Eureka tea plant contain antioxidants that can help protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation, providing relief from upset stomach.
Seeds: The seeds of the Eureka tea plant are rich in essential oils and fatty acids that can help soothe an upset stomach by reducing inflammation and promoting digestion.
Buds: The buds of the Eureka tea plant contain high levels of tannins, which can help reduce inflammation and provide relief from upset stomach symptoms.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers, 1 teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves, and 1 cup of water.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solid plant material.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day as needed.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Rosemary tea helps with upset stomach because it contains anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic compounds, such as rosmarinic acid and carnosol. These compounds help soothe the digestive tract, reduce inflammation, and alleviate muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal system, thereby providing relief from symptoms such as nausea, bloating, and cramps. Furthermore, rosemary tea has carminative properties, which help expel gas from the stomach, reducing discomfort and pain. Additionally, it promotes the production of bile, which aids in the digestion of fats, making it an effective remedy for indigestion and related issues.
remedy_properties: Antispasmodic: Rosemary tea can help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing spasms and cramps that can cause discomfort and pain in an upset stomach. This property is due to the presence of volatile oils such as cineol, camphene, and borneol.

Carminative: Rosemary tea promotes the expulsion of intestinal gas, helping to alleviate bloating, discomfort, and pain associated with an upset stomach. The active constituents responsible for this effect are volatile oils, including alpha-pinene and limonene.

Anti-inflammatory: Rosemary tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract, providing relief from symptoms of an upset stomach. The main active constituents responsible for this effect are rosmarinic acid and carnosol.

Choleretic: Rosemary tea stimulates the production of bile in the liver, which can help improve digestion and reduce symptoms of an upset stomach. The active constituents that contribute to this effect include rosmarinic acid and ursolic acid.

Antioxidant: Rosemary tea has antioxidant properties that can help protect the gastrointestinal tract from oxidative stress and damage, promoting overall gut health. The primary active constituents responsible for this effect are carnosol, rosmarinic acid, and ursolic acid.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the rosemary tea plant are commonly used to make medicinal tea for upset stomach due to their anti-inflammatory and calming properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the rosemary tea plant contain essential oils that can help soothe an upset stomach and promote digestion.
Stems: The stems of the rosemary tea plant have a mild flavor and can help stimulate digestion and alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach.
Seeds: The seeds of the rosemary tea plant are rich in antioxidants and can help soothe an upset stomach and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried rosemary leaves to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the rosemary leaves.
Sip the hot tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your upset stomach.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marjoram tea helps with upset stomach because it contains antispasmodic and carminative properties that work together to soothe the digestive tract, reducing cramping and gas. Its volatile oils, including terpinen-4-ol and sabinene hydrate, have been shown to relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal system, alleviating discomfort and promoting normal digestion. Furthermore, marjoram tea's anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects can help protect the stomach lining from irritation and potential infections, making it an ideal natural remedy for easing an upset stomach.
remedy_properties: Carminative: Marjoram tea helps to relieve stomach discomfort by promoting the expulsion of gas from the digestive tract, reducing bloating and pain. This property is due to the presence of volatile oils such as terpinene, carvacrol, and limonene.

Antispasmodic: Marjoram tea helps to alleviate abdominal cramps and spasms associated with upset stomach by relaxing the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract. This is attributed to the tea's content of flavonoids and tannins, which have muscle-relaxing and anti-inflammatory effects.

Antiemetic: Marjoram tea can help to control nausea and vomiting, common symptoms of an upset stomach. This is due to the presence of compounds like carvacrol and thymol, which have been shown to have antiemetic properties.

Digestive stimulant: Marjoram tea promotes the production of digestive enzymes, facilitating the breakdown of food and the absorption of nutrients. This is important for upset stomach as it helps to maintain a healthy digestive process and prevents further irritation. The tea's essential oils, such as eugenol and linalool, contribute to this property.

Anti-inflammatory: Marjoram tea has anti-inflammatory effects, which can help soothe and heal irritated stomach lining. This property is attributed to the tea's content of phenolic acids, flavonoids, and tannins, which can help reduce inflammation and provide relief from stomach discomfort.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the marjoram tea plant are commonly used in medicinal tea due to their antispasmodic and carminative properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of an upset stomach.
Flowers: The flowers of the marjoram tea plant contain volatile oils that have been shown to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help soothe an upset stomach.
Seeds: The seeds of the marjoram tea plant contain high levels of essential oils, which can help stimulate the digestive system and provide relief for an upset stomach.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, place one teaspoon (2 grams) of dried marjoram leaves.
Pour 8 ounces (240 milliliters) of boiling water over the marjoram leaves.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes to extract the herb's properties.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the used marjoram leaves.
Drink the warm marjoram tea to soothe an upset stomach, sipping it slowly.
